text:
Carabid Beetle (ゴミムシ型, Gomimushi-gata—lit. "Garbage Insect/Carabid Beetle-type") Hunter × Hunter - Episode 100 (2011) is a Chimera Ant and former Captain of Leol's Squad who carries the genes of the bombardier beetle and a dung beetle. He ambushes Killua along with other Ants under Flutter's command. Hunter × Hunter - Volume 23, Chapter 236 

Appearance

He is a beetle-like Chimera Ant with a rather anthropomorphic look. He is bald, with two antlers on his head and two much longer ones protruding from his scapulae. His body is a light shade of beige in color, except for his head and fingers, which are white. He also has a black shell on each shoulder. He wears maroon pants with a belt. 

Plot

Chimera Ant arc

Carabid Beetle tries to roll a giant dung-ball at Killua, but realizes that the boy is far too powerful for him to face and tries to flee. Killua pursues him, only for the Ant to use its special ability on him. His whereabouts are currently unknown.

Abilities & Powers

As a Chimera Ant, he is stronger than normal humans. He can also stand and run atop a huge ball of dung which, due to its weight and speed, has enough kinetic energy to bring down trees standing in its path. 

Enhanced Durability: His body is considerably durable, since a punch from a Nen-using Rammot Hunter × Hunter - Volume 19, Chapter 198 did not leave him with any permanent or visible injury.

Nen

After being violently "initiated", Hunter × Hunter - Volume 19, Chapter 198 Carabid Beetle learned Nen. Hunter × Hunter - Volume 20, Chapter 204 

Manga Appearances

Battles

Trivia

The character's true name was never given in the manga, and the 2011 anime adaptation only identifies him by his type of species in the credits.
He is referred to as ゴミムシ (Gomimushi, literally "Garbage Insect"; a diminutive for ミイデラゴミムシ, Mīderagomimushi, translated as "Pheropsophus jessoensis"—colloquially known as the "Asian bombardier beetle").
In the credits for the English dub: "Carabid Beetle"; referring to the family of insects also known as Carabidae or Ground beetles—a large group that includes the bombardier beetles.

Miscellaneous

The image illustrating the explanation of a bombardier beetle's defense mechanism in Chapter 236 is based on a photo taken by Satoshi Kuribayashi, a Japanese photographer.
